html:
Harald Hildetand ("battle-tooth"), whose Old West Norse name is Haraldr hilditönn, is a mythological king of Svitjod, Denmark, Norway, and the lands of the Vandals. He was the son of Hrörek of Lejre from the Sköldunga clan and Aud Djupaudga, daughter of Ivar Vidfamne. According to other sources (Sögubrot and Hyndlas song), he was the son of Radbard of Gårdarike. He is said to have grown up in Gårdarike. After his maternal grandfather died, he returned to Scania, from where he conquered Svitjod, Denmark, Norway, and northern Germany. He is said to have resided at the fortress of Beritzholm, near the present-day Bjärsjölagårds castle. His brother, Randver, ruled over Northumbria. When he was old, he made his half-brother Randver's son, Sigurd Ring, a sub-king over Svitjod. When Harald realized that he was getting old, 150 years old, and did not want to die from the plague, he challenged Sigurd Ring to a confrontation in the legendary Battle of Bråvalla. The sources about Harald Hildetand are Saxo Grammaticus's *Gesta Danorum* and some Icelandic sagas, primarily *Hervararsaga*.

== References ==

== External links == Harald Hildetand in Nordisk familjebok (second edition, 1909)

How certain can we be about this?

Harald Hildetand is known almost exclusively from sagas and Saxo Grammaticus, recorded 300–400 years after his supposed time. No contemporary sources confirm him. Information about enormous armies is unreasonable for the Iron Age of Scandinavia. Today, he is largely considered to be legendary.
tldr: Harald Hildetand is a king from the old Norse sagas who is said to have ruled over vast areas in Scandinavia and Germany. Today, however, he is considered to be a mythical figure rather than a real historical one.
lattlast:
Harald Hildetand is described in old stories as a powerful king who ruled over areas that today comprise Denmark, Norway, and parts of Germany. He is said to have been a warrior who conquered vast territories and lived in a castle in Scania.

Stories about him are mainly preserved in ancient sagas and books written long after he supposedly lived. Since there is no scientific evidence for his existence, he is now considered a figure from mythology rather than a real historical person.

Certain details in the stories, such as claims that he was 150 years old or led enormous armies, are considered unlikely given the time period he is said to have lived in. Therefore, while he is an important figure in Nordic culture and literature, he is not a confirmed historical individual.
